title: The Summit and the Strait: Trump-Xi Align on Hormuz
subhead: Trump and Xi agreed the Strait of Hormuz 'must remain open' while BRICS fractures over Iran and new ship attacks near Oman underscore the fragility of any cease-fire. Europe's AccelerateEU plan reveals €24 billion in energy costs with no additional supply.

sections.heading: Trump-Xi Summit: China Offers to Help on Iran
sections.body:
President Trump and Chinese leader Xi Jinping met in Beijing on Thursday, issuing a joint statement that the Strait of Hormuz 'must remain open' — the first explicit US-China alignment on the critical waterway since the Iran war began. Xi reportedly offered to help Trump negotiate with Tehran, positioning China as a potential mediator while protecting its own energy interests.

The summit came as US efforts to end the Iran war showed signs of strain. Trump warned that a Middle East cease-fire is on 'life support,' while Iran stated it is 'ready for any aggression.' A ship was seized off the UAE coast and is heading toward Iranian waters, and an Indian-flagged cargo vessel sank off Oman following a suspected drone or missile strike — the latest reminders that the maritime conflict continues despite diplomatic efforts.

China's involvement reflects its dependence on Middle East energy: Beijing has been drawing down strategic oil reserves during the Hormuz disruption, cutting imports from the region while leaning on Russian supplies and domestic coal. US LNG vessels have resumed China-bound shipments after a year-long pause, signaling a tactical energy détente even as broader trade tensions persist.
sections.whyItMatters: China's offer to mediate gives Tehran a potential diplomatic off-ramp while preserving Beijing's energy access — but it also positions China as indispensable to any resolution. The trap for Washington is accepting Chinese mediation without securing Iranian commitments, while China gains influence over Gulf security architecture.

sections.heading: BRICS Divided: Iran Demands Condemnation, UAE Resists
sections.body:
BRICS foreign ministers gathered in New Delhi on Thursday as the bloc's expansion has exposed deep fractures over the Iran war. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi urged member states to condemn the US and Israel over 'unlawful aggression,' while one member country — reportedly the UAE — pushed for language condemning Iran, preventing a unified position.

India's External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar called for BRICS to address 'unilateral coercive measures and sanctions inconsistent with international law' — a pointed reference to US sanctions that have complicated India's energy imports. With the US waiver on Russian oil imports set to expire on May 16, India is seeking an extension that American officials have indicated will not come.

Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov attended, representing the other major sanctioned power in the grouping. The meeting highlighted the tension between BRICS' ambition to represent the Global South and the competing interests of its members: Iran and the UAE are both BRICS members pursuing opposing regional agendas.
sections.whyItMatters: BRICS' inability to unified on Iran reveals the limits of anti-Western coalitions when member states have conflicting security interests. The decisive move for BRICS would be developing crisis-specific mechanisms that accommodate member differences, but the Iran war has exposed the fault line between energy importers and regional rivals.

sections.heading: India's Russian Oil Waiver Expires Saturday
sections.body:
India's waiver to import Russian oil expires on May 16, with US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent indicating there will be no extension. India's imports of Russian crude surged to 1.96 million barrels per day in May — up from 1.57 million in April — as refiners rushed to secure supplies before the deadline.

Jaishankar criticized 'unilateral' sanctions at the BRICS meeting, calling them 'unjustified' and noting they 'disproportionately affect developing countries.' India's traditional position is to reject non-UN sanctions, though in practice it has complied with US sanctions for commercial reasons.

Two India-bound LPG tankers successfully crossed the Strait of Hormuz despite the ongoing maritime conflict, but an Indian-flagged cargo vessel sank off Oman after a suspected drone or missile attack. India condemned the attack as 'unacceptable,' highlighting the risks to its energy logistics even as it navigates sanctions pressure from Washington.
sections.whyItMatters: India faces a structural choice between energy security via discounted Russian crude and strategic alignment with Washington. The waiver expiration forces a decision point — either India finds alternative sources at higher cost, or it accepts sanctions risk to maintain energy access. Either path carries diplomatic costs.

sections.heading: Europe's AccelerateEU: €24 Billion in Extra Costs, No Extra Energy
sections.body:
The European Commission announced AccelerateEU, a plan to address the energy crisis caused by the Iran war. The EU has spent an additional €24 billion on energy imports since the Hormuz closure — but has not received 'a single extra molecule of energy' due to price surges, according to the Commission's press release.

The plan aims to provide immediate relief to households and industries while accelerating the transition to 'clean, secure, and affordable energy.' European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen called energy independence a 'security imperative,' while Energy Commissioner Dan Jørgensen warned that Europe faces 'yet another fossil energy crisis.'

Central and Eastern European states face particular strain. Tamara Lagurashvili of the Clean Air Task Force noted these countries have 'tight fiscal space and long-term overreliance on fossil fuel imports.' Hungary's Viktor Orbán and Slovakia's Robert Fico had called for easing sanctions on Russian energy — but Hungary's April election replaced Orbán with Péter Magyar, who aims to end Russian influence in Hungary, leaving Fico isolated.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe's energy bills are rising without corresponding supply gains — a structural vulnerability that no amount of diversification can solve quickly. The decisive move is accelerating domestic generation while securing alternative suppliers, but the gap between spending and supply reveals the limits of money in a supply-constrained market.

sections.heading: US LNG Dominance: Two-Thirds of Europe's Imports by 2026
sections.body:
The US is set to become Europe's largest LNG supplier in 2026, with American gas accounting for nearly two-thirds of European LNG imports, according to IEEFA forecasts. Between 2021 and 2025, Europe's US LNG imports more than tripled as countries reduced dependence on Russian pipeline gas.

The EU announced plans to phase out Russian LNG by the end of 2026, extending the energy sanctions regime that began after Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine. Yet Russian LNG imports to the EU rose 16 percent in early 2026 — a paradoxical outcome where Europe pays more for American gas while continuing to purchase Russian supplies.

European regulators have warned that swapping Russian dependence for American dependence creates its own vulnerabilities. Alessia Virone of the Clean Air Task Force noted that 'overreliance on a single import partner, fuel, or technology creates systemic vulnerabilities during geopolitical crises.' The AccelerateEU plan explicitly aims to reduce this concentration risk through electrification and domestic generation.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe has traded one concentrated dependency for another. The decisive move would be building regasification capacity and supplier diversity in parallel, not sequentially — but infrastructure takes years. The trap is assuming US gas abundance persists indefinitely while neglecting the infrastructure for alternative sources.

sections.heading: Gulf Divergence: Saudi Aramco Surges While Qatar Faces Years of Recovery
sections.body:
The Iran war has exposed a sharp divergence within the Gulf Cooperation Council. Saudi Aramco reported a 26% jump in first-quarter profits as the company leveraged its east-west pipeline to export 7 million barrels per day through the Red Sea port of Yanbu, bypassing Hormuz entirely. The UAE similarly pushed crude through its Fujairah pipeline to the Gulf of Oman.

Qatar and Kuwait have no such alternatives. Iranian strikes on Qatar's Ras Laffan industrial complex have paralyzed its LNG exports, with QatarEnergy estimating it will take up to five years to repair the damage. Around 80 energy facilities across the Gulf have been targeted, with damage estimated at $58 billion.

The World Bank downgraded GCC growth forecasts from 4.4% to 1.3% for 2026, with Oxford Economics predicting some Gulf economies will enter recession in the second half. Tourism and aviation — pillars of GCC diversification — have also suffered: hotel occupancy in Dubai is projected to fall to 10% in Q2 from 80% before the war.
sections.whyItMatters: Geography and infrastructure investment determined Gulf states' fates. Saudi Arabia and the UAE can export around Hormuz; Qatar and Kuwait cannot. The decisive move for energy importers is identifying which suppliers have genuine resilience versus those claiming it — and diversifying accordingly.

sections.heading: Türkiye Energy Angle: Central Bank Warns of War-Driven Inflation
sections.body:
Türkiye's central bank raised its 2026 year-end inflation target from 16% to 24%, warning that the 'short-term inflationary effects of the Iran war will remain pronounced.' Governor Fatih Karahan cited the closure of the Strait of Hormuz as a risk to global energy supply, noting rising input costs and supply chain disruptions.

Türkiye's annual energy inflation rose by 19 percentage points to 47%, driven primarily by oil and natural gas prices. Despite challenges, exports increased in April while imports excluding gold and energy declined, narrowing the trade deficit. The current account deficit-to-GDP ratio remains below historical averages.

The central bank held its key interest rate at 37%, with Karahan stating 'all options are on the table' for policy response. The bank shifted from forecast band communication to point forecasts, reflecting increased uncertainty. Energy price volatility has become 'the primary factor in the recent inflation outlook.'
sections.whyItMatters: Türkiye's energy import dependency makes it acutely vulnerable to Hormuz disruption. The decisive move is accelerating the middle corridor strategy — Caspian energy via Azerbaijan — while the southern route remains contested. The trap is assuming monetary policy alone can manage imported inflation without structural energy diversification.

bottomLine: The Trump-Xi summit produced a rare point of US-China alignment — both want Hormuz open — but the underlying maritime conflict continues to claim vessels and reshape energy markets. India's Russian oil waiver expires Saturday, forcing New Delhi to choose between energy security and sanctions compliance. Europe is spending billions more for the same energy while BRICS fractures over Iran. The Gulf's divergence is stark: Saudi Aramco surges on pipeline exports while Qatar faces years of LNG recovery. The decisive insight is that infrastructure determines resilience — states that invested in bypass routes before the crisis are now profiting from them, while those that assumed open seas endured are paying the price. Every energy decision in 2026 is being made against a backdrop of chokepoint risk that cannot be priced away, only mitigated through physical alternatives.
sources: Los Angeles Times, Forbes, The Hindu, Daily Sabah, The Conversation, Reuters, IEEFA, CNBC, Firstpost, CBS News, Financial Times, World Bank, Oxford Economics, Clean Air Task Force, European Commission
